skill.name: adme-property-predictor
skill.description: Predict Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, and Excretion properties for drug candidate molecules using molecular descriptors and cheminformatics models.
skill.summary: Cheminformatics tool that predicts ADME properties and drug-likeness scores from molecular structure inputs.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer detected 121 potential issues across 5 scanned files (1526 lines), including weak cryptographic algorithm references, system reconnaissance patterns, shell backtick execution, and network calls. After thorough manual evaluation, all 121 findings are confirmed as false positives. The weak cryptographic algorithm flags are RDKit chemical descriptor function names (MolWt, MolLogP, TPSA) and pharmaceutical documentation terms. The system reconnaissance flags are standard ADME terminology. The shell backtick executions are Markdown code formatting. The hardcoded URL is a source attribution link. No prompt injection attempts, malicious patterns, or actual security threats were found. The skill is a legitimate ADME property prediction tool for pharmaceutical research with proper input validation and no data exfiltration capabilities.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 39 instances of 'weak cryptographic algorithm' across scripts/main.py, SKILL.md, and references/runtime_checklist.md. In main.py, the flagged code includes RDKit molecular descriptor calls (MolWt, MolLogP, TPSA) and Cheminformatics library imports from rdkit.Chem - these are legitimate scientific computing functions, not cryptography. In SKILL.md, flagged text includes pharmaceutical documentation terms (predict, model, score, rules) which are domain-specific ADME vocabulary, not cryptographic algorithm references. In adme-property-predictor_audit_result_v2.json, the flag is on evaluation result data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern-based scanner misidentified scientific computing function names and domain-specific documentation as cryptographic algorithms. All flagged code uses standard scientific libraries (RDKit) and pharmaceutical terminology.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 18 instances of 'system reconnaissance' across SKILL.md, scripts/main.py, and adme-property-predictor_audit_result_v2.json. The flagged text includes standard pharmaceutical documentation terms (validate, check, model, system) used in ADME prediction context, as well as SMARTS pattern matching code for chemical substructure identification in main.py. These are legitimate cheminformatics operations and documentation, not system reconnaissance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner keyword-matched pharmaceutical terminology (validate, system, model, check) as reconnaissance indicators. All terms are used in legitimate drug discovery documentation and chemical structure analysis code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Shell Backtick Execution Detection
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged 79 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' primarily in SKILL.md and references/runtime_checklist.md. The flagged backtick characters are standard Markdown formatting for inline code and code fences (```python, ```bash, ```text). These are documentation formatting elements, not shell command execution. SKILL.md uses backticks extensively for YAML front matter, Python code examples, bash command examples, and text formatting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Scanner confused Markdown backtick code formatting with Ruby shell backtick command execution. SKILL.md is a documentation file using standard Markdown syntax for code blocks and inline code references.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL / Network Detection
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged the source repository link in SKILL.md as a hardcoded URL/network call. The URL at line 7 is a source attribution: https://github.com/aipoch/medical-research-skills. This is a documentation link, not executable network code. The Python script (main.py) makes no network calls or HTTP requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged URL is a static documentation link in Markdown format. No network libraries (urllib, requests, http) are imported or used anywhere in the skill code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: File Write Operation Detection
low_findings.description: Static scanner flagged open(args.output, 'w') at scripts/main.py line 483 as a file system write operation. This is legitimate output file generation controlled by the user via the command-line --output argument. The script writes ADME prediction results in JSON format to a user-specified path, which is expected behavior for a CLI data analysis tool.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: File write is controlled by explicit user-supplied command-line argument (args.output). No unauthorized or hidden file writes exist. Standard behavior for a CLI data processing tool.

content.user_title: Predict ADME Properties of Drug Candidates
content.value_statement: Drug discovery teams often lack rapid computational tools to evaluate absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion properties of candidate molecules. This skill provides cheminformatics-based ADME prediction using molecular descriptors and drug-likeness scoring from a single SMILES input.
actual_capabilities: Predict absorption properties including Caco-2 permeability and human intestinal absorption percentage
Calculate drug-likeness scores using Lipinski Rule of 5 and Veber rule filters
Predict distribution, metabolism, and excretion properties from molecular structure
Process single compounds or batch libraries for high-throughput screening
Generate structured ADME reports in JSON or table format
Rank and recommend candidates based on multi-parameter optimization scoring
limitations: Predictions are computational estimates and do not replace experimental ADME studies
Models are trained on small molecules (MW 100-800 Da) and are unreliable for biologics or novel scaffolds
Predictions assume physiological pH 7.4 and may not account for disease states or drug interactions
Static models do not capture time-dependent changes like enzyme induction or inhibition

use_cases.title: Early-Stage Compound Screening
use_cases.description: Medicinal chemists evaluate new synthetic compounds for oral bioavailability and drug-likeness before committing to expensive assays.

use_cases.title: Academic Drug Research
use_cases.description: Academic researchers assess ADME profiles of novel molecules to prioritize candidates for publication and further study.

use_cases.title: Candidate Prioritization
use_cases.description: Drug discovery teams rank compound libraries by multi-parameter scores to select the most promising candidates for experimental validation.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Single Compound Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user running a single compound analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Predict ADME properties for Aspirin. Its SMILES is CC(=O)Oc1ccccc1C(=O)O. Give me the full ADME report with absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion results.

prompt_templates.title: Batch Library Screening
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher analyzing multiple drug candidates from a file
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ADME Property Predictor to analyze the compounds in library.smi. Screen for oral bioavailability and rank the candidates by drug-likeness score.

prompt_templates.title: Custom Property Filtering
prompt_templates.scenario: Medicinal chemist filtering compounds for specific ADME criteria
prompt_templates.prompt: Run ADME prediction on these 5 SMILES compounds. Filter for candidates with HIA above 80 percent, no Lipinski violations, and half-life between 2 and 8 hours.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Parameter Optimization
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced user optimizing for a CNS drug target profile
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform multi-parameter optimization on the compounds in candidates.csv. Use a CNS target profile requiring high BBB penetration, PPB under 90 percent, and high metabolic stability. Show the top 5 ranked candidates with scores.

output: Absorption: Lipinski Pass (0 violations), HIA 96%, Caco-2 high permeability, soluble class
Distribution: Not BBB permeable, plasma protein binding 55%, volume of distribution 0.35 L/kg
Metabolism: CYP3A4 substrate, CYP2C9 non-substrate, moderate metabolic stability
Excretion: Half-life 3.5 hours, clearance 8.2 mL/min/kg, mixed renal/hepatic route
Drug-likeness Score: 0.85 out of 1.0 - Excellent drug candidate

output_examples.input: Compare ADME profiles of Ibuprofen and Naproxen
output_examples.output: A side-by-side analysis showing both compounds pass Lipinski rules with good oral absorption. Ibuprofen shows a shorter half-life of 2 hours and lower plasma protein binding of 60 percent. Naproxen has a longer half-life of 14 hours and higher binding at 95 percent. Naproxen achieves a higher drug-likeness score due to favorable half-life. Both are recommended as good candidates with minor concerns.

output_examples.input: Screen compound library of 150 molecules for CNS drug candidates
output: 66 compounds passed Lipinski and Veber rule filters for oral bioavailability
12 compounds show BBB permeability suitable for CNS targets
Top 3 candidates ranked by multi-parameter optimization score with full ADME profiles attached below
All 12 CNS-permeable candidates recommended for experimental validation

best_practices: Validate SMILES strings before prediction to ensure the correct molecular structure is analyzed
Use predictions for compound prioritization and always confirm top candidates with experimental assays
Compare results from multiple prediction models to build consensus and reduce model-specific bias
anti_patterns: Treating computational ADME predictions as definitive experimental measurements for Go/No-Go decisions
Applying rigid cutoff values to drug-likeness scores without considering project-specific requirements
Using prediction models on molecular structures far outside the training set applicability domain

faq.question: What is ADME prediction?
faq.answer: ADME stands for Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, and Excretion. These are key pharmacokinetic properties that determine how a drug moves through the body. This skill predicts these properties from the molecular structure to assess drug-likeness.

faq.question: What input format does this skill accept?
faq.answer: The skill accepts SMILES strings for individual compounds and CSV files with a SMILES column for batch processing. SMILES is a standard text notation for representing molecular structures.

faq.question: How accurate are the predictions?
faq.answer: Accuracy varies by property. LogP predictions are very good (R-squared 0.85 to 0.95). Solubility and metabolism predictions are moderate (R-squared 0.60 to 0.80). Predictions guide prioritization but do not replace experimental data.

faq.question: Can this skill process large compound libraries?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports batch processing with parallel execution for libraries up to 10,000 compounds. Processing time ranges from minutes for hundreds of compounds to about one hour for 10,000 compounds on a 4-core machine.

faq.question: What drug-likeness rules are implemented?
faq.answer: The skill implements Lipinski Rule of 5, Veber rules for oral bioavailability, and QED (Quantitative Estimation of Drug-likeness) scoring. Multi-parameter optimization scoring is also available for custom target profiles.

faq.question: How should I validate the predictions?
faq.answer: Compare predictions to existing experimental data for similar compounds. Check that predicted values are internally consistent, for example high LogP should correlate with low solubility. Always confirm top candidates with in vitro or in vivo assays.
